caveats: a: The BLS occupation mix covers all truck transportation and excludes self-employed workers.
a: O*NET task frequencies are occupation-wide rather than specific to long-distance truckload fleets.
a: Driverless feasibility is highly operating-domain-specific and should not be interpreted as exposure of every lane or duty.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
rho: Autonomy-provider scale targets and readiness statements are forward-looking and may slip.
rho: Implementation varies sharply by weather, road class, terminal access, cargo, state law, and customer process.
rho: Back-office deployment is easier but represents a smaller wage pool than driving.

e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a sector margin and may misclassify firms near the earnings-band boundaries.
e: A carrier may fit the service definition yet fail practical transferability because of safety history, customer concentration, or owner dependence.
s5: Neither source measures truckload-carrier control transfers in the target EBITDA band.
s5: Marketplace transactions skew smaller than the target band and are voluntarily reported.
s5: Industry downturns can increase seller intent while reducing the share of businesses that close as going concerns.
q: Large public-carrier disclosures are a proxy for LMM pricing behavior.
q: Dedicated, differentiated, or capacity-constrained lanes can retain more than commodity one-way truckload.
q: The estimate excludes implementation probability and freight-volume response to lower rates.

d5: Real industry output is not identical to constant-quality physical service quantity.
d5: Truckload demand is cyclical and can deviate sharply from long-run forecasts over one five-year window.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Shippers and logistics intermediaries purchase full-load capacity under lane or customer contracts and spot quotes, generally priced per mile, shipment, or committed capacity with fuel and accessorial adjustments.
lens.excluded_activities: Private shipper fleets, captive transportation units, freight brokers without carrier operations, independent driving services classified elsewhere, local trucking, LTL networks, specialized freight, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market for-hire carriers providing recurring or repeat long-distance full-truckload movement of general freight from shipper origin to consignee destination.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation shares for all truck transportation at NAICS 484000, not long-distance truckload alone. The mix is combined with O*NET task content and observed limited-domain driverless freight: portions of highway driving, route planning, logs, document review, dispatch, billing, customer contact, and management analysis are treated as exposed, while loading interfaces, inspections, securing freight, maintenance, adverse-weather or off-domain driving, and physical exception response are not. Including local and specialized trucking likely understates long-haul highway exposure, while treating mature corridor demonstrations as evidence of broader task exposure may overstate it.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix covers all truck transportation and excludes self-employed workers.
O*NET task frequencies are occupation-wide rather than specific to long-distance truckload fleets.
Driverless feasibility is highly operating-domain-specific and should not be interpreted as exposure of every lane or duty.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis as stated in the assignment.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 484121 occupational special tabulation plus time-and-motion data from representative truckload fleets separating highway driving, surface streets, loading and waiting, inspections, paperwork, dispatch, maintenance, sales, and finance would materially revise the range.
a.rationale: Heavy tractor-trailer drivers are 58.12% of truck-transportation employment and office/administrative support is another 11.32%, including dispatchers at 2.42%. Driverless freight is now commercially real on limited routes, showing that a large share of highway driving is technically exposed, while O*NET shows substantial remaining physical, inspection, cargo, and exception tasks. Administrative workflows add broad document, scheduling, pricing, billing, and communication exposure. The range is wage-weighted task exposure, not the share of jobs immediately removable.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all truck transportation, not 484121 service quantity, to grow from $391.5 billion in 2024 to $487.1 billion in 2034, a 2.2% compound annual rate. FHWA separately forecasts combination-truck vehicle miles to increase 1.1% annually through 2050 and FAF projects truck tonnage at 1.2% annually from 2017 to 2050. These broader measures are translated to a five-year truckload quantity range, with a base below the BLS real-output implication because price/mix, brokerage, local, LTL, and specialized activity differ from long-distance general TL. The resulting error can run either direction.
caveats: All cited forecasts cover broader truck or freight populations than the target code.
Real industry output is not identical to constant-quality physical service quantity.
Truckload demand is cyclical and can deviate sharply from long-run forecasts over one five-year window.
d5.change_evidence: Build a 484121-specific panel of loaded miles, loads, tons, deadhead, and constant-quality revenue from target carriers and benchmark it against FAF origin-destination commodity forecasts, shipper contract volumes, industrial production, imports, and intermodal lane forecasts.
d5.rationale: Physical goods production and consumption continue to require line-haul freight, and all three federal proxies indicate positive long-run truck activity. Cyclic inventories, trade policy, nearshoring, intermodal competition, fleet productivity, and a possible recession create meaningful five-year dispersion. A moderate real quantity increase is the central case rather than extrapolation of any single forecast.

caveats: Eligibility is not observed for the 2,819 modeled firms.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a sector margin and may misclassify firms near the earnings-band boundaries.
A carrier may fit the service definition yet fail practical transferability because of safety history, customer concentration, or owner dependence.
e.change_evidence: Construct the modeled firm list from Census and FMCSA records and verify operating authority, safety status, tractor count, customer concentration, independent-contractor mix, normalized EBITDA, and whether each company can transfer control without loss of authority or material contracts.
e.rationale: The assigned count is drawn from private employer firms in the target earnings band, while the NAICS definition itself is a for-hire, origin-to-destination full-load service. That makes recurring or repeat external service the dominant model. The deduction covers inactive or misclassified records, predominantly captive relationships, firms whose normalized earnings do not survive diligence, and operations too dependent on one owner, authority, customer, or leased asset base to transfer as a going concern.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 484121 ($34,122,891,072) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 484121 ($172,857,094,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1558 (margins.json[484]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Trucking EBITDA/Sales 15.58%); revenue/employee $291,906 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 484121); class <5: 46,822 firms, avg revenue/firm $456,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $71,072 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,713 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,581,853 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $402,253 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,225 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,973,761 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $774,912 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,294 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,002,066 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,869,922 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 525 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,589,987 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,947,120 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 326 firms, avg revenue/firm $245,099,988 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,186,578 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,819

caveats: Operator-required does not mean a human must remain in the cab.
Private-fleet insourcing would reduce the lens share even though physical trucking persists.
Intermodal substitution is primarily reflected in d5 to avoid double counting.
o.change_evidence: Track shipper private-fleet conversion, autonomous carrier and DaaS contracting structures, FMCSA operating-authority rules for ADS carriers, intermodal conversion by lane, and target customers' five-year make-versus-buy plans.
o.rationale: Software cannot move a full truckload; even a driverless tractor must be owned, dispatched, maintained, insured, monitored, and operated under accountable motor-carrier safety oversight. FMCSA explicitly states that entities responsible for ADS-equipped CMVs remain motor carriers subject to oversight. Some shipper insourcing, intermodal conversion, or software-mediated capacity reclassification could remove quantity from the lens, but the great majority of surviving service still requires a carrier operator.

caveats: Large public-carrier disclosures are a proxy for LMM pricing behavior.
Dedicated, differentiated, or capacity-constrained lanes can retain more than commodity one-way truckload.
The estimate excludes implementation probability and freight-volume response to lower rates.
q.change_evidence: Review target-carrier bid calendars, rate sheets, lane awards, fuel and accessorial formulas, customer contracts, gross margin by lane, autonomous-service pricing, and renewal outcomes following measurable productivity changes; interview shipper procurement teams about should-cost treatment.
q.rationale: Truckload is fragmented and price-led, with thousands of small carriers and both contractual and spot quotes. That structure passes a large part of visible cost reductions to shippers at bids, renewals, and market resets. Operators may retain benefits from superior utilization, service reliability, proprietary lane density, or temporarily scarce autonomous capacity, but technology vendors may also capture part through DaaS or equipment pricing. Discounted five-year retention is therefore below half in the central case.

caveats: Autonomy-provider scale targets and readiness statements are forward-looking and may slip.
Implementation varies sharply by weather, road class, terminal access, cargo, state law, and customer process.
Back-office deployment is easier but represents a smaller wage pool than driving.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain lane-level mileage and origin-destination data for target carriers, then match it to validated autonomous operating domains, OEM delivery schedules, DaaS pricing, insurance quotes, terminal requirements, remote-support ratios, and actual labor hours removed in commercial deployments.
rho.rationale: Routine back-office and dispatch-assistance uses should diffuse broadly within five years, but the larger exposed driver pool will convert slowly. Current commercial driverless revenue and fleets remain tiny relative to national truckload capacity; deployment requires supported corridors, compatible tractors and terminals, remote assistance, maintenance, insurance, customer acceptance, and reliable handoff for off-domain work. LMM fleets are more likely to access autonomy through a vendor or OEM service than develop it, and supplier pricing may delay adoption even where technically feasible.

s5.bridge: The Exit Planning Institute's cross-industry U.S. owner survey found 49% wanted to exit within five years. That intention is discounted for delays, failed processes, closures, asset sales, family or employee transitions without qualifying external control, and trucking-specific financing, insurance, safety, and customer-consent barriers. Broad small-business marketplace evidence on completed sales and retirement motivation is used only as directional support. Selection into exit surveys may overstate near-term intent, while the large owner-operator heritage of trucking may increase succession pressure.
caveats: Neither source measures truckload-carrier control transfers in the target EBITDA band.
Marketplace transactions skew smaller than the target band and are voluntarily reported.
Industry downturns can increase seller intent while reducing the share of businesses that close as going concerns.
s5.change_evidence: Use FMCSA and commercial ownership data to identify the modeled firms, then obtain owner age, succession plans, transaction history, liens, fleet age, safety records, and five years of closed control deals from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, SBA loan data, and trucking-specialist brokers.
s5.rationale: The industry has a deep independent carrier population and recurring consolidation, so qualifying opportunities should arise across a modeled pool of 2,819 firms. Still, wanting to exit is materially different from closing a control transaction, and cyclic freight earnings, equipment debt, claims, authority, and customer concentration impair completion. The base therefore sits far below stated five-year exit intent.

principal_driver: Limited-domain driverless freight makes a large driver wage pool technically exposed, while a deep carrier population supplies many potential transferable firms.
principal_weakness: Most exposed driver work will not be implemented quickly across smaller fleets, and competitive freight pricing plus technology-vendor economics can pass away much of the realized benefit.

sources.supports: Broader-industry occupation mix, including heavy tractor-trailer drivers at 58.12%, office/administrative support at 11.32%, and dispatchers at 2.42% of employment.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: NAICS 484000 Truck Transportation

sources.supports: Driver tasks include driving, route adjustment, logs and document review as well as vehicle and cargo inspection, coupling, loading support, maintenance, and emergency work.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Heavy and Tractor-Trailer Truck Drivers

sources.supports: Dispatch work includes scheduling operations and employees, coordinating resources, relaying information, responding to problems, and maintaining records.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Dispatchers, Except Police, Fire, and Ambulance

sources.supports: Reports $1 million of Q1 2026 revenue across driverless and supervised commercial loads, continued testing of weigh-station and on-route fueling, and planned second-generation driverless hardware rollout.
sources.title: Aurora Innovation First Quarter 2026 Shareholder Letter

sources.supports: Explains Level 4 operation within an operating domain, continued human roles outside it, regulatory adaptation needs, and continuing motor-carrier safety oversight even without an onboard human.
sources.title: FMCSA: Safe Integration of Automated Driving Systems-Equipped Commercial Motor Vehicles

sources.supports: States that little research had focused specifically on ADS-equipped commercial vehicles and describes ongoing Level 3 and Level 4 transfer-of-control research.
sources.title: FMCSA: Human Factor in ADS-Equipped Commercial Motor Vehicles

sources.supports: Defines the industry as long-distance full-truck movement of general freight from origin to destination, with a single full load not combined with other shipments.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 484121 General Freight Trucking, Long-Distance, Truckload

sources.supports: Reports 50,747 employer firms, $170.831 billion of sales or revenue, 590,026 employees, and $34.507 billion of annual payroll for the six-digit industry.
sources.title: 2022 Annual Business Survey: Employer Firms in NAICS 484121

sources.supports: Reports that 49% of surveyed U.S. business owners wanted to exit within five years.
sources.title: 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report

sources.supports: Reports 2,117 completed small-business transactions in Q2 2026, retirement as the leading stated sale motivation at 45%, and financing and preparation constraints on completion.
sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report: Q2 2026

sources.supports: Describes full-load freight as highly fragmented and competitive, with thousands of often-small carriers, competition primarily on price, service and capacity, and use of both contractual and spot rate quotes.
sources.title: J.B. Hunt Transport Services 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Projects truck-transportation real output from $391.5 billion in 2024 to $487.1 billion in 2034, a 2.2% compound annual increase.
sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Forecasts combination-truck vehicle miles to increase 1.1% annually through 2050.
sources.title: FHWA 2024 Forecasts of Vehicle Miles Traveled

sources.supports: Projects truck freight tonnage from 11.848 billion tons in 2017 to 17.545 billion tons in 2050, a 1.2% compound annual increase.
sources.title: FHWA Freight Analysis Framework Version 5 Overview

title: General Freight Trucking, Long-Distance, Truckload
